content:
# Genesis Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11v–12r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_021_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_021_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 16–17 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** The LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did to Sarah as he had promised.

**2** And Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age at the appointed time of which God had spoken to him.

**3** Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore him, Isaac.

**4** And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him.

**5** Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born to him.

**6** And Sarah said, "God has made laughter for me; everyone who hears will laugh with me."

**7** And she said, "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age."

**8** And the child grew and was weaned. And Abraham made a great feast on the day that Isaac was weaned.

**9** But Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, laughing.

**10** So she said to Abraham, "Cast out this slave woman and her son, for the son of this slave woman shall not be heir with my son Isaac."

**11** And the thing was very displeasing to Abraham on account of his son.

**12** But God said to Abraham, "Be not displeased on account of the boy and on account of your slave woman. Whatever Sarah says to you, listen to her voice, for through Isaac shall your offspring be named.

**13** And I will also make a nation of the son of the slave woman, because he is your offspring."

**14** So Abraham rose early in the morning and took bread and a skin of water and gave it to Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, along with the child, and sent her away. And she departed and wandered in the wilderness of Beer-sheba.

**15** When the water in the skin was gone, she put the child under one of the bushes.

**16** Then she went and sat down opposite him a good way off, about the distance of a bowshot, for she said, "Let me not look on the death of the child." And as she sat opposite him, she lifted up her voice and wept.

**17** And God heard the voice of the boy, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven and said to her, "What troubles you, Hagar? Fear not, for God has heard the voice of the boy where he is.

**18** Rise up, lift up the boy and hold him fast with your hand, for I will make him into a great nation."

**19** Then God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. And she went and filled the skin with water and gave the boy a drink.

**20** And God was with the boy, and he grew up. He lived in the wilderness and became an expert with the bow.

**21** He lived in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt.

**22** At that time Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army said to Abraham, "God is with you in all that you do.

**23** Now therefore swear to me here by God that you will not deal falsely with me or with my children or with my descendants. As I have dealt kindly with you, so you will deal with me and with the land where you have sojourned."

**24** And Abraham said, "I will swear."

**25** When Abraham reproved Abimelech about a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized,

**26** Abimelech said, "I do not know who has done this thing. You did not tell me, and I have not heard of it until today."

**27** So Abraham took sheep and oxen and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a covenant.

**28** Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock apart.

**29** And Abimelech said to Abraham, "What is the meaning of these seven ewe lambs that you have set apart?"

**30** He said, "These seven ewe lambs you will take from my hand, that this may be a witness for me that I dug this well."

**31** Therefore that place was called Beer-sheba, because there both of them swore an oath.

**32** So they made a covenant at Beer-sheba. Then Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army rose up and returned to the land of the Philistines.

**33** Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beer-sheba and called there on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God.

**34** And Abraham sojourned many days in the land of the Philistines.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 21 spans Folios 11v–12r.

   **Folio 11v** (GEN_021_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 11. The chapter opens in the right-hand column of this folio, which closes the Genesis 20 material at its top before Genesis 21 begins in the same column. The opening verses of Genesis 21 — the divine fulfillment of the promise to Sarah (vv. 1–2), the birth and naming of Isaac (vv. 3–5), Sarah's laughter-song (vv. 6–7), and the weaning feast narrative (vv. 8–9) — are clearly legible in the Leningrad Codex's characteristic three-column layout with Masoretic pointing and te'amim. The Hebrew text of the chapter heading, the paragraph markers (setumah and petuchah), and the verse-dividing accents are all visible. The narrative continues through the expulsion of Hagar (vv. 10–14) and the wilderness episode (vv. 15–21), running to approximately the midpoint of the folio.

   **Folio 12r** (GEN_021_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 12. This folio carries the remainder of Genesis 21 — Abimelech's covenant with Abraham at Beer-sheba (vv. 22–34) — followed by the opening verses of Genesis 22 (the binding of Isaac) in the subsequent columns. The chapter conclusion at verses 31–34 is clearly visible, with the Beer-sheba etymology (v. 31), the covenant confirmation (v. 32), the tamarisk tree planting (v. 33), and the closing notice of Abraham's sojourn in the land of the Philistines (v. 34). The Masoretic paragraph division (petuchah) between Genesis 21 and 22 is legible.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 16 (GEN_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the Genesis 20–21 transition, with the chapter marker "CAP. XXIII" visible in the top margin — indicating Vaticanus's section enumeration for what corresponds to Genesis 21. Page 17 (GEN_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 21 and the opening of Genesis 22, with the three-column uncial hand of Vaticanus clearly legible throughout.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיְהוָה פָּקַד אֶת שָׂרָה — "The LORD Visited Sarah" (v. 1) — The Verb פָּקַד**

The chapter opens with a divine fulfillment notice in two parallel clauses:

וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת שָׂרָה כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמָר
וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה לְשָׂרָה כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר
(vaYHWH paqad et Sarah ka'asher amar
vaYHWH ya'as leSarah ka'asher dibber)
"The LORD visited Sarah as he had said,
and the LORD did to Sarah as he had promised."

The verb פָּקַד (paqad) is one of the richest and most contested terms in the Hebrew Bible. Its semantic range spans: to visit, to attend to, to muster, to appoint, to oversee, to remember with care. In contexts of divine grace toward barren women, it carries the specific sense of a purposeful divine attention that results in conception — the same verb is used of God "visiting" Hannah (1 Samuel 2:21) and is part of the Joseph narrative's anticipation of divine salvation (Genesis 50:24-25). The formal translation renders "visited" (following ESV, NASB, KJV) rather than "gracious to" (NRSV) in order to preserve the active, personal, visiting character of the divine action. God does not merely bestow favor from a distance; he comes and acts.

The doubling of the clause with two different verbs (paqad / ya'as — "visited / did") and two different words for speaking (amar / dibber — "said / promised/spoke") creates an emphatic rhetorical completeness: what was announced has been accomplished, in every word of its announcement.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: καὶ κύριος ἐπεσκέψατο τὴν Σαρραν (kai kyrios epeskepsato ten Sarran, "and the Lord visited Sarah") — using ἐπισκέπτομαι (episkeptomai, "to visit, to look upon with care") for פָּקַד, in exact correspondence. This is the same Greek verb used in Luke 1:68, 78 for God's messianic visitation, establishing a verbal link between the Abrahamic promise and the New Testament fulfillment.

**2. יִצְחָק — "Isaac" and the Tsechoq / Yitschaq Wordplay (vv. 3–9)**

The name Isaac — יִצְחָק (Yitschaq) — derives from the root צָחַק (tsachaq, "to laugh"). The chapter is saturated with this root:

- **v. 6**: Sarah says אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה לִי צְחֹק (Elohim asah li tsechoq, "God has made laughter/laughing for me")
- **v. 6**: כָּל הַשֹּׁמֵעַ יִצְחַק לִי (kol hashome'a yitschaq li, "everyone who hears will laugh with/for me") — the verb form יִצְחַק (yitschaq) is phonetically identical to the name Isaac
- **v. 9**: וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת בֶּן הָגָר... מְצַחֵק (vatere Sarah et ben Hagar... metsacheq, "Sarah saw the son of Hagar... laughing/mocking")

The wordplay is irreducibly present in the Hebrew: the name of the child of promise (יִצְחָק, Yitschaq) is sounded again whenever the root appears. The formal translation cannot fully reproduce this, but retains "laughter" (v. 6), the verb "laugh" (v. 6), and "laughing" (v. 9) to preserve as much of the sonic resonance as English allows.

**3. צְחֹק עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים — "God Has Made Laughter for Me" (v. 6) — Sarah's Second Laughter**

Sarah's laughter has transformed. In Genesis 18:12, she laughed inwardly in skeptical disbelief when she overheard the divine promise: "After I am worn out, and my lord is old, shall I have pleasure?" The divine rebuke followed (18:13–15). Now, in 21:6, her laughter has become something entirely new — it is the laughter of fulfilled joy, of vindicated faith, of impossible promise kept:

אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה לִי צְחֹק
כָּל הַשֹּׁמֵעַ יִצְחַק לִי
(Elohim asah li tsechoq
kol hashome'a yitschaq li)
"God has made laughter for me;
everyone who hears will laugh with me."

The preposition לִי (li, "for me / to me / with me") in the second clause is ambiguous in Hebrew: it can mean "will laugh on my behalf / with me in my joy" or, less favorably, "will laugh at me" (in mockery of the absurdity of an old woman nursing). Both readings are linguistically valid. The formal translation adopts the joyful reading — "laugh with me" — which fits the context of Sarah's exultation and is supported by the Vaticanus LXX: συγχαρεῖταί μοι (synchareitas moi, "will rejoice with me"), using a compound verb (syn + chairein, "to rejoice together with") that definitively reads the laughter as shared joy, not ridicule.

The second line of verse 7 — "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age" — is the only place in the Hebrew Bible where Sarah speaks in her own voice about the miracle of her son's birth. The formal translation preserves the rhetorical question form as the Hebrew has it.

**4. מְצַחֵק — Metsacheq: The Ambiguity of Ishmael's Action (v. 9)**

Verse 9 is one of the most theologically contested verses in the chapter:

וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת בֶּן הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם מְצַחֵק
(vatere Sarah et ben Hagar hamitsrit asher yaldah leAvraham metsacheq)
"But Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, laughing."

The participle מְצַחֵק (metsacheq, Piel of צָחַק) is the same verbal root as Isaac's name. The question of what precisely Ishmael was doing when Sarah saw him has generated centuries of interpretive debate:

- **Simple translation**: He was "laughing" — playing, rejoicing, being a child — in which case Sarah's reaction is driven by jealousy or rivalry over inheritance, not by any offense.
- **"Mocking"**: The Piel stem of צָחַק can carry an intensified or negative force compared to the Qal — "mocking" or "scoffing." Some interpreters read Ishmael as ridiculing Isaac specifically. This is the reading implied by the KJV ("mocking") and many patristic writers.
- **Sexual implication**: In a handful of other biblical passages the Piel of צָחַק appears to carry sexual overtones (Genesis 26:8, where Isaac is "laughing with" his wife Rebekah; Genesis 39:14, 17, where Potiphar's wife accuses Joseph of "playing with / mocking" her). If so, the scene would be more sinister.
- **The LXX addition**: The Vaticanus LXX adds μετὰ Ισαακ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς (meta Isaak tou huiou autes, "with Isaac her son") — specifying that Ishmael was "playing with" or "mocking" Isaac. This addition is not in the MT. Paul's use of this text in Galatians 4:29 ("just as at that time he who was born according to the flesh persecuted him who was born according to the Spirit") may depend on this LXX tradition.

The formal translation renders the neutral "laughing" (the plain denotation of the Hebrew participle), following the MT without the LXX addition, while noting the interpretive range in these notes.

**5. גָּרֵשׁ — "Cast Out" (v. 10) — Sarah's Demand**

Sarah's demand is expressed with the strong verb גָּרַשׁ (garash, "to drive out, to expel, to cast out forcibly") — the same verb used for the expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:24: "he drove out the man") and for the driving out of the Canaanite nations (Exodus 23:28–31). The formal translation renders "cast out" (KJV, ESV) rather than the softer "send away" (NIV) in order to preserve the forceful character of Sarah's demand.

The legal point underlying Sarah's demand — "the son of this slave woman shall not be heir with my son Isaac" — is supported by ancient Near Eastern legal practice. The Code of Hammurabi (LH 170–171) distinguishes between the inheritance rights of sons born to a wife from those born to a slave woman in the event of the slave woman's sons being claimed as heirs: if a father acknowledges them as sons, they inherit; if not, the slave woman and her sons receive their freedom but no share of the estate. Sarah's appeal is to the principle that the acknowledged-heir's inheritance must not be divided with the slave-woman's son.

The divine ratification of Sarah's demand in verse 12 is theologically striking: God tells Abraham to listen to Sarah's voice in this matter, explicitly grounding the decision in the line of promise — "through Isaac shall your offspring be named."

**6. כִּי בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע — "Through Isaac Shall Your Offspring Be Named" (v. 12)**

This verse is quoted verbatim by Paul in Romans 9:7 and Galatians 4:30, making it one of the most theologically consequential verses in Genesis. The Hebrew:

כִּי בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע
(ki veYitschaq yiqqare' lekha zara')
"For through Isaac shall your offspring be named."

The preposition בְּ (be-, "in / through / by means of") before Yitschaq (Isaac) can be rendered either "in Isaac" (a locative sense — within Isaac's line) or "through Isaac" (an instrumental sense). Paul in Romans 9:7 cites the LXX: ἐν Ισαακ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα (en Isaak klethesetai soi sperma, "in Isaac shall your seed be called"), using ἐν (en) for the Hebrew בְּ — and his argument in Romans 9 depends on distinguishing between natural descent from Abraham and the specific line of divine calling and promise. The formal translation renders "through Isaac" to convey the instrumental force.

**7. הַגָּר וְהַיֶּלֶד בַּמִּדְבָּר — Hagar and the Child in the Wilderness (vv. 14–19)**

The wilderness episode is one of the most humanly affecting passages in Genesis. Several translation points:

- **v. 14**: Abraham "put it on her shoulder, along with the child" — the Hebrew וְאֶת הַיֶּלֶד (ve'et hayyeled, "and the child") is in apposition to the things Abraham placed on Hagar's shoulder, which has troubled interpreters (Ishmael is by this time a teenager if the chronology is followed). The formal translation follows the most natural reading of the Hebrew syntax as presented in the Leningrad Codex.

- **v. 16**: "about the distance of a bowshot" — כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת (kimitchavey qeshet), a vivid approximation of distance by the range of an arrow-shot. The formal translation preserves this precise idiom.

- **v. 17**: "God heard the voice of the boy" — this is the fulfillment of the name Ishmael (יִשְׁמָעֵאל, Yishma'el, "God hears / God will hear"). The name announced in Genesis 16:11 — "you shall call his name Ishmael, because the LORD has listened to your affliction" — is now semantically enacted in the phrase שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶל קוֹל הַנַּעַר (shama' Elohim el qol hanna'ar, "God heard the voice of the boy"). The formal translation preserves this verbal precision.

- **v. 17**: "What troubles you, Hagar?" — מַה לָּךְ הָגָר (mah lakh Hagar, literally "What is to you, Hagar?" or "What ails you, Hagar?"). The divine question echoes the identical question asked of Hagar by the angel in Genesis 16:8. The formal translation renders "What troubles you?" as the most natural English equivalent.

**8. בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע — Beer-sheba: Well of the Oath or Well of Seven (v. 31)**

The place-name etymology:

עַל כֵּן קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בְּאֵר שָׁבַע
כִּי שָׁם נִשְׁבְּעוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם
(al ken qara' lamaqom hahu Beer Sheva
ki sham nishbe'u sheneihem)
"Therefore that place was called Beer-sheba,
because there both of them swore an oath."

The name בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע (Be'er Sheva) is a pun with two equally valid etymological interpretations:

1. **"Well of the Oath"**: בְּאֵר (be'er, "well") + שֶׁבַע from שָׁבַע (shava', "to swear"). This is the etymology the narrator gives in verse 31: they swore (nishbe'u) there. The verb נִשְׁבְּעוּ (nishbe'u) contains the same root שׁ-ב-ע (shin-bet-ayin) as שֶׁבַע.

2. **"Well of Seven"**: בְּאֵר (be'er, "well") + שֶׁבַע (sheva', "seven") — pointing to the seven ewe lambs (שֶׁבַע כְּבָשׂוֹת, sheva' kevashot) that Abraham set apart as the token of the covenant (vv. 28–30). The root שׁ-ב-ע is shared by both "seven" and "swear" (an oath was commonly sealed by invoking seven, or by sacrificing seven animals).

The narrator activates both meanings simultaneously through the narrative: the seven lambs and the oath are both present in the scene. The formal translation renders "Beer-sheba" as the place name, following the narrator's explicit etymological note in verse 31 ("because there both of them swore an oath"), while noting in these notes that the "well of seven" pun is equally operative.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: Φρέαρ ὁρκισμοῦ (Phrear horkismou, "well of the oath/swearing") — choosing only the "oath" etymology, losing the "seven" wordplay.

**9. אֵשֶׁל — Tamarisk Tree (v. 33)**

אֵשֶׁל (eshel) is generally identified as the tamarisk tree (Tamarix aphylla or a related species), a long-lived tree native to the Negeb and southern Canaan. The tamarisk is deep-rooted, drought-resistant, and associated with permanence — its planting here by Abraham at Beer-sheba signals the establishment of a lasting sacred site, not a transient encampment. The formal translation renders "tamarisk tree" (ESV, NASB) rather than "grove" (KJV, which misread the term) or the generic "tree."

**10. יְהוָה אֵל עוֹלָם — "The LORD, the Everlasting God" (v. 33)**

The divine title אֵל עוֹלָם (El Olam) — "the Everlasting God" or "the God of Eternity / the God of Antiquity" — appears here for the only time in Genesis as a title invoked at a specific location. The noun עוֹלָם (olam) spans a semantic field from "long duration" to "eternity" to "the distant past / ancient time." The compound title El Olam emphasizes the timelessness and the enduring constancy of the God Abraham is calling upon — a God whose faithfulness extends across all ages. The formal translation renders "the Everlasting God" (ESV, NASB) as the most precise English equivalent.

The invocation is at Beer-sheba, the location where Abraham has just made a covenant. The planting of the tamarisk tree and the calling on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God, together establish Beer-sheba as a lasting cultic and covenantal site — it will recur throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 22:19; 26:23–33; 28:10; 46:1).

The Vaticanus LXX renders: θεὸν αἰώνιον (theon aionion, "the eternal God" or "the everlasting God") — using αἰώνιος (aionios, "eternal, age-long"), the same adjective that will carry enormous theological weight in the New Testament (αἰώνιος ζωή, "eternal life"). The LXX translation captures both the temporal endlessness (eternity) and the antiquity (everlastingness) of the Hebrew olam.

**11. פִּיכֹל — Phicol, Commander of the Army (vv. 22, 32)**

The name פִּיכֹל (Phicol) is a non-Semitic name that recurs in Genesis 26:26 (where Phicol accompanies Abimelech again to make a covenant with Isaac). The recurrence of the same names across two generations (or more) is one of the patriarchal narrative's puzzles — whether Phicol and Abimelech are the same individuals in both stories or whether the names represent dynastic titles is not resolved by the text itself. The formal translation renders the name as it appears in the Leningrad Codex without harmonizing.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 21

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 16–17 (GEN_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The LXX of Genesis 21 is largely consistent with the MT but has several notable variants:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 21:1 | פָּקַד (paqad, "visited/attended to") | ἐπεσκέψατο (epeskepsato, "visited, looked upon") | "visited" | LXX exactly corresponds; both preserve the active personal visitation |
| 21:6 | יִצְחַק לִי (yitschaq li, "will laugh with/at me") | συγχαρεῖταί μοι (synchareitas moi, "will rejoice with me") | "laugh with me" | LXX explicitly chooses the joyful "laugh with" reading, ruling out "laugh at"; formal translation follows this interpretation |
| 21:9 | מְצַחֵק (metsacheq, "laughing") | παίζοντα μετὰ Ισαακ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς (paizonta meta Isaak tou huiou autes, "playing with Isaac her son") | "laughing" | LXX adds "with Isaac her son" — not in MT; formal translation follows MT, notes LXX addition |
| 21:12 | בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע ("through Isaac shall your offspring be named") | ἐν Ισαακ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα ("in Isaac shall your seed be called") | "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" | LXX cited by Paul in Rom 9:7; formal translation follows MT's instrumental sense |
| 21:14 | וְאֶת הַיֶּלֶד ("and the child") | καὶ τὸ παιδίον ("and the child") | "along with the child" | Both agree; the syntactic difficulty (child on shoulder?) is present in both traditions |
| 21:17 | שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים ("God heard") | εἰσήκουσεν ὁ θεός ("God heard") | "God heard" | Exact agreement; Ishmael name-fulfillment present in both |
| 21:21 | מִדְבַּר פָּארָן ("wilderness of Paran") | ἔρημον Φαραν ("wilderness of Pharan") | "wilderness of Paran" | Exact agreement |
| 21:31 | בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע ("Beer-sheba") | Φρέαρ ὁρκισμοῦ ("well of the oath") | "Beer-sheba" | LXX interprets rather than transliterates; formal translation follows MT place name with the "oath" etymology noted in v. 31 |
| 21:33 | אֵל עוֹלָם ("the Everlasting God") | θεὸν αἰώνιον ("the eternal God") | "the Everlasting God" | LXX uses αἰώνιος, the same adjective as NT "eternal"; formal translation renders "Everlasting God" |

The most theologically significant LXX variant in Genesis 21 is the addition at verse 9 — μετὰ Ισαακ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς ("with Isaac her son") — which frames Ishmael's metsacheq as specifically directed at or shared with Isaac, rather than simply a general activity that Sarah happens to observe. This addition, which underlies Paul's reading in Galatians 4:29 ("he who was born according to the flesh persecuted him who was born according to the Spirit"), materially affects the interpretation of the episode and demonstrates the theological shaping of the LXX tradition.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 21 — Four Episodes**

The chapter divides into four clearly demarcated narrative units:

1. **vv. 1–7**: The birth and naming of Isaac — fulfillment of the divine promise
2. **vv. 8–21**: The weaning feast, the expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael, and their preservation in the wilderness
3. **vv. 22–32**: Abimelech's covenant with Abraham at Beer-sheba
4. **vv. 33–34**: The planting of the tamarisk tree and the invocation of YHWH El Olam

These four episodes are held together by the dominant wordplay on צָחַק / יִצְחָק (tsachaq / Yitschaq — laugh / Isaac) in the first two units, and by the Beer-sheba location in the final two. The chapter traces the movement from the birth of the child of promise to the establishment of a covenant site that will anchor the Abrahamic narratives through Genesis 26.

**The Ishmael Narrative — Structure and Contrast**

Genesis 21:8–21 is in deliberate structural parallel with Genesis 16 (the first Hagar narrative):

| Genesis 16 | Genesis 21 |
|---|---|
| Hagar flees (v. 6) | Hagar is sent away (v. 14) |
| Hagar in the wilderness (v. 7) | Hagar in the wilderness of Beer-sheba (v. 14) |
| Angel of the LORD finds her by a spring (v. 7) | God hears the voice of the boy; angel calls from heaven (v. 17) |
| "Return and submit" (v. 9) | "Rise up, lift up the boy" (v. 18) |
| Promise: "I will multiply your offspring" (v. 10) | Promise: "I will make him a great nation" (v. 18) |
| Name prophecy: Ishmael (v. 11) | Name enacted: "God heard the voice of the boy" (v. 17) |
| "You are a God of seeing" — El Roi (v. 13) | God opens Hagar's eyes (v. 19) |

The formal translation preserves the verbal parallels between the two passages in order to honor this narrative architecture.

**The Divine Name Pattern — Elohim Throughout**

Like Genesis 20, Genesis 21 employs Elohim (אֱלֹהִים) as the dominant divine name throughout the narrative sections (vv. 2, 4, 6, 11, 12, 17, 19, 20, 22, 23), with YHWH appearing at structurally significant moments: the fulfillment notice (vv. 1a, 1b), the Beer-sheba covenant climax (v. 33). The formal translation renders Elohim as "God" and YHWH as "the LORD" consistently throughout.
